top_categories.workout_context: 63ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Race-pace efforts that mimic K2 Road Cycle Classic's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before November.
At K2 Road Cycle Classic, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
At mile 60 of 119, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
K2 Road Cycle Classic's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 119 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of K2 Road Cycle Classic. Train it until it feels boring.
7,546ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
7,546ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 7,546ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At K2 Road Cycle Classic, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Low-cadence force work for 7,546ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on mixed terrain. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for K2 Road Cycle Classic’s 119 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.terrain: Mixed terrain at K2 Road Cycle Classic requires surface adaptability. Include weekly rides on mixed terrain to build confidence.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Race Simulation, and Over Under to prepare you for 119 miles of mixed terrain in Coromandel Peninsula, New Zealand.
